deals_cleared.merger_description: Genea Holdco SA Pty Limited (**Genea**) (via a newly created special purpose vehicle Genea Bidco SA Pty Ltd) proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in Embrace Fertility Pty Ltd (**Embrace Fertility**). In return, the owner of Embrace Fertility will hold a minority equity interest in Genea (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Genea - Embrace Fertility - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Genea Holdco SA Pty Limited (Genea) (via a newly created special
purpose vehicle Genea Bidco SA Pty Ltd) proposes to acquire 100%
of the shares in Embrace Fertility Pty Ltd (Embrace Fertility)
(collectively referred to as the Parties). In return, the owner of
Embrace Fertility will hold a minority equity interest in Genea (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

Genea owns and operates an IVF and fertility business called Fertility
SA that provides assisted reproduction services via a clinic in
Adelaide, South Australia, as well as clinics in other states.
Embrace Fertility is an independent gynaecology and IVF and fertility
business providing assisted reproduction services in Adelaide, South
Australia.
Embrace Fertility does not have its own IVF laboratory and
historically Repromed has provided laboratory services to Embrace
Fertility, however, Genea would provide these services to Embrace
Fertility going forward.

The Parties overlap in the supply of IVF and fertility services in
Adelaide, South Australia.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and engagement with third parties.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• The merged firm would continue to face competition from
alternative suppliers of IVF and fertility services in Adelaide.
• The Acquisition does not materially change the existing
dynamics given Embrace Fertility would continue to use
laboratory services of another provider (historically Repromed
but going forward Genea).

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: WIN Television Network Pty Ltd (**WIN TV**) proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in NBN Enterprises Pty Limited (**NBN**) from Nine Network Australia Holdings Pty Ltd and 100% of the shares in Television Holdings Darwin Pty Limited (**NTD**) from Nine Network Australia Pty Limited (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Questionnaire - WIN Television Network - NBN Enterprises & Television Holdings Darwin
events.display_title: Questionnaire - WIN Television Network - NBN Enterprises & Television Holdings Darwin
events.url_gh: /mergers/MN-50008/WIN Network - NBN Enterprises and Television Holdings Darwin - Third party questionnaire.pdf

events.title: WIN Television Network - NBN Enterprises & Television Holdings Darwin - Phase 1 Determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
WIN Television Network Pty Ltd (WIN TV) proposes to acquire 100%
of the shares in NBN Enterprises Pty Limited (NBN) from Nine
Network Australia Holdings Pty Ltd and 100% of the shares in
Television Holdings Darwin Pty Limited (NTD) from Nine Network
Australia Pty Limited (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, WIN TV, is the parent company of a group of wholly
owned subsidiaries with broadcast free-to-air television in different
licence areas throughout regional Australia. WIN TV acquires the
majority of its content for broadcast through an affiliate relationship
with the Nine Group.
The targets, NBN and NTD, operate regional free-to-air television
stations, with NBN broadcasting across the Northern New South
Wales and Gold Coast region and NTD broadcasting to Darwin,
Palmerston and the surrounding areas in the Northern Territory. Both
NBN and NTD currently broadcast a combination of content
produced by the Nine Group, together with locally produced content.

There is no geographic overlap between the local broadcasting
areas of WIN TV, NBN and NTD.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third parties, and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• There is no overlap between the Parties as they operate in
different geographic areas.
• The Parties would likely continue to face competition from
multiple alternative suppliers of broadcasting and advertising in
those regional areas in Australia, including by Seven Network
and Ten Network.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Gilead Sciences, Inc. (Gilead), through its wholly owned subsidiary Ojas Merger Sub LLC, proposes to acquire all of the outstanding shares of Ouro Medicines, LLC (Ouro) (the Acquisition).

events.title: Gilead - Ouro - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Gilead Sciences, Inc. (Gilead)’s proposed acquisition, via its wholly
owned subsidiary Ojas Merger Sub LLC, of all of the outstanding
shares of Ouro Medicines, LLC (Ouro) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Gilead, is a biopharmaceutical company incorporated
in the United States. Globally, Gilead’s activities focus on advancing
medicines to prevent and treat life-threatening diseases, including
HIV, liver disease, coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19),
inflammation and cancer. Its primary subsidiary in Australia is Gilead
Sciences Pty. Ltd., through which Gilead supplies Australian
customers with a range of medicines. Gilead does not have any
manufacturing or R&D facilities in Australia but runs clinical trials in
Australia and employs an operations team in Australia to support
those trials.
The target, Ouro, is a privately-held, U.S.-based clinical-development
stage biotechnology company focused on developing T cell engager
(TCE) therapies for autoimmune diseases.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
Gilead and Ouro (together, the Parties) are both active in the
development of T cell therapies for the treatment of autoimmune
diseases.
Gilead is engaged in Phase 1 clinical and preclinical research and
development pipeline activities for CAR-T early-stage programs.
Ouro is engaged in Phase 1b clinical development of TCE therapies.
Given the early stage of development of the Parties’ T cell therapies
for the treatment of autoimmune diseases, potential indications for
treatment are not known at this stage, with the exception of
idiopathic inflammatory myopathy, where there is some overlap in
the Parties’ pipeline products.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third partes and publicly available
information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• The potential competition between the Parties in the supply of T
cell therapies is uncertain, given the early stages of the Parties’
respective clinical trials and lack of identified overlapping
potential indications.
• There are other competitors active in the development of CAR-T
and TCE therapies that will continue to compete with Gilead.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: GIP V Snow Bidco Limited (the **Acquirer**) proposes to acquire 100% of the issued share capital of Envol Holdings Limited and its subsidiaries including TCR Group (the **Target**) (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Global Infrastructure Management - TCR – Phase 1 determination

determination_table_content.details:
GIP V Snow Bidco Limited proposes to acquire 100% of the issued
share capital of Envol Holdings Limited and its subsidiaries including
TCR Group.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, GIP V Snow Bidco Limited (the Acquirer) proposes to
acquire 100% of the issued share capital of Envol Holdings Limited
and its subsidiaries including TCR Group (the Target)
(the Acquisition).
The Acquirer is a special purpose vehicle controlled by funds
managed and/or advised by Global Infrastructure Management, LLC
(together with is affiliates, Global Infrastructure Management).
Global Infrastructure Management is the global independent
infrastructure fund manager of Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP),
headquartered in New York, United States (US), and primarily
focused on investing in transportation, energy, waste, water, and
digital infrastructure sectors. Relevantly to the Acquisition, Global
Infrastructure Management also holds a 37% interest in Sydney
Aviation Alliance Holdings Pty Ltd, which fully owns Sydney Airport
Corporation Ltd. (Sydney Airport). Sydney Airport owns small
amounts of GSE with which it provides services to airlines. Global
Infrastructure Management is ultimately controlled by BlackRock,
Inc. (BlackRock).
BlackRock is a US company listed on the New York Stock Exchange.
BlackRock is active in the provision of global investment
management, risk management and advisory services to
institutional and retail clients around the world.
The target is the holding entity of TCR Group (TCR), which is
primarily active in full-service leasing of ground support equipment
(GSE) to independent ground handlers, airlines, and airports globally.
TCR also provides repair and maintenance services for GSE (on both
leased and third-party fleet) and fleet management services. TCR is
headquartered in Steenokkerzeel, Belgium.
In Australia, TCR is involved in the leasing out and maintenance of
GSE at airports around Australia through various subsidiaries.
TCR is currently indirectly solely controlled by 3i Group plc. 3i Group
plc is an international investment company listed on the London
Stock Exchange, with offices in Europe and North America.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
There are vertical relationships between Global Infrastructure
Management and TCR relating to services at Sydney Airport. The
parties also overlap in the supply of GSE services to airlines at
Sydney Airport.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information submitted by third parties and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• Noting a concern was raised by a third party that the Acquisition
may reduce competitors’ access to space at Sydney Airport, the
merged firm would be unlikely to have the ability to foreclose
rivals’ access to inputs at Sydney Airport as it does not have
sufficient control over the entities that ultimately control Sydney
Airport so as to foreclose rivals’ access and Sydney Airport
would not have the incentive to foreclose rivals’ access.
• The merged firm would likely continue to be constrained by
alternative suppliers of GSE services.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_name: Quadrant Private Equity - Stanley International College
deals_cleared.merger_description: Swan Topco Pty Ltd proposes to acquire 100% of the issued share capital in Stanley International College Pty Ltd (**Stanley International College**) by an acquisition vehicle, Swan Bidco Pty Ltd, that is indirectly and solely controlled by funds managed by Quadrant Private Equity (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Woodward pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Swan Topco Pty Ltd (Swan Topco), through its indirect wholly-owned
subsidiary Swan Bidco Pty Ltd (Swan Bidco), applied for a
notification waiver in respect of Swan Bidco’s proposed acquisition
of 100% of the issued share capital in Stanley International College
Pty Ltd (Stanley International College) from Chintalia Pty Ltd and
Kaishwer Pty Ltd (together, the Vendors), as described in the
transaction documents provided as part of the application (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Swan Bidco, is indirectly controlled by funds managed
by Quadrant Private Equity (QPE). QPE is a private equity firm
headquartered in Sydney, Australia, and supplying financial and
investment management services across Australia and New
Zealand. It typically invests in mid-market companies across
consumer and retail, healthcare, business, professional and
industrial services, as well as technology and tech-enabled service
industries.
The target, Stanley International College, is an independent institute
of higher education and vocational education and training services. It
employs over 200 staff and is a multi-campus institution with a
presence in Perth and Adelaide.
Stanley International College provides a range of vocational training,
bachelor and post-graduate degrees and professional year
qualifications.
Quadrant Private Equity’s portfolio companies do not, and have not
previously, held any interests in any higher education, vocational
education or training service providers.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular, there is no horizontal overlap between
QPE’s portfolio companies and Stanley International College in the
supply of higher education and/or vocational education training
services.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: The Acquisition involves the merger of Indovida India Private Limited (**Indovida India**) with and into EPL Limited (**EPL**), by way of absorption of Indovida India as a going concern. In consideration of the merger, EPL (as the post-merger resultant entity) will issue and allot shares on a proportionate basis to the shareholders of Indovida India. As a result of the Acquisition, Indorama Netherland B.V. (**INBV**) (an indirect wholly owned subsidiary of Indorama Ventures Public Company Limited (**IVL**)) and Blackstone Inc. (**Blackstone**, which exercises sole control over Epsilon Bidco Pte. Ltd. (**Epsilon**), the existing controlling shareholder of EPL) will acquire indirect joint control over EPL. As a consequence of the Acquisition, INBV's direct interest in EPL will increase from 24.51% to approximately 52%.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Woodward pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
EPL Limited (EPL) applied for a notification waiver in respect of its
proposed acquisition of 100% of the issued share capital of Indovida
India Private Limited (Indovida India) and its proposed subsidiaries,
through a share-for-share exchange, as described in the transaction
documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, EPL, is a publicly listed Indian company engaged in the
manufacturing and sale of packaging products, including laminated
and extruded plastic tubes, caps and closures, and laminates. Its
products are supplied to customers in fast-moving consumer goods
and pharmaceutical industries, including in Australia.
The target, Indovida India, and its proposed subsidiaries (described
below) (together, the Indovida Group) form part of the wider IVL
Group, a global conglomerate headed by Indorama Ventures Public
Company Limited. It has diversified business interests, including in
plastic packaging.
Prior to completion of the Acquisition, various subsidiaries of
Indovida Netherlands B.V. will be consolidated under Indovida India.
Subsequently, the Indovida Group will comprise Indovida India and
its proposed subsidiaries, which are engaged in the manufacture and
sale of pre-forms, bottles, and high-density polyethylene (HDPE) and
polypropylene (PP) closures for use in the food and beverage and
consumer products sectors globally. The Indovida Group’s activities
in Australia are limited to the supply of imported packaging products,
specifically pre-forms made from polyethylene terephthalate, and
closures made from HDPE and PP.
The parties are therefore both active in the supply of plastic
packaging products in Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
certain publicly available information, and had regard to the factors
in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010
(Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is limited overlap between the parties in the supply of
plastic packaging products in Australia, as their respective
offerings serve different use cases
b. the parties each have a small Australian presence
c. there are alternative suppliers of the types of plastic
packaging products offered by the parties in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_name: AXDI - minority interest in La Trobe Financial
deals_cleared.merger_description: AXDI SPV 5 Ltd (**AXDI**) proposes to acquire a minority interest in AMC AusCo 1 Ltd (the **Target**) from BCP AMC Aus LP (by its general partner Brookfield Capital Partners VI GP LLC, by its sole member Brookfield Capital Partners VI Officer GP LLC) (**Brookfield**), by way of a share sale agreement.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
AXDI SPV 5 Ltd (AXDI) applied for a notification waiver in respect of
its proposed acquisition of a minority interest in AMC AusCo 1 Ltd
(the holding company of La Trobe Financial) (the Target) from BCP
AMC Aus LP (by its general partner Brookfield Capital Partners VI GP
LLC, by its sole member Brookfield Capital Partners VI Officer GP
LLC) (Brookfield), as described in the transaction documents
provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, AXDI, is a newly incorporated holding entity managed
by Axight Capital Limited (Axight), established for the sole purpose
of acquiring a minority interest in the issued share capital of the
Target. Axight is an Abu Dhabi-based private equity-focused
investment manager with over US$5 billion in assets under
management and a focus on investing in developed and developing
markets across Asia Pacific.
The Target is the holding company of La Trobe Financial, an
Australian alternative asset manager with over A$22 billion in assets
under management, specialising in finance and credit investment
solutions. La Trobe Financial’s core offering is Australian Real Estate
Private Credit, being the provision of residential and commercial
loans originated through independent mortgage broker channels in
Australia. It also offers:
a. an ASX-listed Private Credit Fund, providing investors with
monthly income and daily liquidity from diversified
Australian real estate private credit and US mid-market
corporate credit, and
b. a US Private Credit Fund, providing both wholesale and retail
Australian investors with access to a curated and unique set
of investment products.
The vendor, Brookfield, operates in the private equity industry,
focusing on acquiring high-quality, cash-generative businesses in
various sectors.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver, and had regard
to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular, there is no horizontal overlap or vertical
relationship between the target or La Trobe Financial and AXDI (or
their portfolio companies) in the supply of residential and
commercial loan or alternative asset management services in
Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required

Barwon Bidco Pty Ltd (Bidco), which is ultimately owned by

notification waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of a
majority interest in Barwon Investment Partners Pty Ltd (Barwon), as
described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, Hillhouse, is a global alternative investment manager
that deploys capital across multiple strategies, including public
equities, private equity, private credit, and real assets. Through its
private equity arm, Hillhouse invests in long-term global
opportunities across the business services, healthcare, consumer,
and industrials sectors.
The target, Barwon, is a specialist fund manager investing in
alternative assets across healthcare property, real estate credit and
global investments, as well as in private equity for institutional and
wholesale investors.
Both parties supply investment management services in Australia,
although their focus is on different asset classes and in different
sectors.

Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).

the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:

(including between their connected entities)

services in Australia

The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.

competition.

notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on

acquirers.name: MAC HoldCo Pty Ltd (an entity which has not been established yet)

deals_cleared.merger_description: A group of investors proposes to acquire 100% of the issued shares in Moorabbin Airport Corporation Pty Ltd (**MAC**), to be held through a holding company. MAC holds the long-term airport lease granted by the Commonwealth in relation to Moorabbin Airport, a metropolitan airport located south-east of the Melbourne CBD. The investors also propose to acquire leasehold interests in various non-aviation commercial properties located at the Moorabbin Airport site (together, the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Questionnaire - MassMutual, Rest, Aware Super and Goodman - Moorabbin Airport
events.display_title: Questionnaire - MassMutual, Rest, Aware Super and Goodman - Moorabbin Airport

events.title: MassMutual, REST, Aware Super and Goodman - Moorabbin Airport – Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Philip Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
The acquisition of 100% of the issued shares in Moorabbin Airport
Corporation Pty Ltd (Moorabbin Airport Corporation) and leasehold
interests in various non-aviation commercial properties at the
Moorabbin Airport site by a group of investors comprised of
MassMutual, REST, Aware Super and Goodman Limited (Goodman)
(together, the Investors) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The Investors comprise:
• MassMutual, a mutual life insurance company based in
Massachusetts, United States;
• REST, an Australian profit-to-member superannuation fund
with approximately 2 million members and around $105
billion in funds under management;
• Aware Super, an Australian superannuation and retirement
fund, with almost 1.2 million members and $198.7 billion in
funds under management; and
• Goodman, part of the Goodman Group, an ASX-listed
industrial property and digital infrastructure business.
Goodman’s primary business activities include the
ownership, development and management of essential real
estate assets, including logistics facilities, warehouses and
data centres worldwide.
The target, Moorabbin Airport Corporation, holds the long-term
airport lease granted by the Commonwealth in relation to Moorabbin
Airport, a metropolitan airport located south-east of the Melbourne
CBD. Moorabbin Airport provides services to facilitate general
aviation activities such as flight training, private air charter, freight,
recreational flying and air work and emergency services. Moorabbin
Airport has only one regular passenger transport service, with King
Island Airlines flying between King Island and the mainland daily.
The Moorabbin Airport site consists of industrial land as well as
aviation land, and contains over 250 mixed-use, large and small
businesses, including Kingston Central Plaza, Direct Factory Outlet
(also known as DFO) and the Chifley Business Park.
Goodman currently holds 100% of the issued shares in Moorabbin
Airport Corporation indirectly.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
The Moorabbin Airport site includes industrial land, and the Investors
have interests in industrial land across Greater Melbourne.
The Moorabbin Airport site also includes aviation land, and the
Investors, excluding Goodman, also hold interests in other airports,
which either focus on different primary operations to Moorabbin
Airport (that is, they are focused on regular passenger transport
rather than general aviation services) or offer general aviation
services outside of Victoria.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and information from third partes.
During consultation, some third parties raised concerns, primarily
about the potential for aviation land surrounding Moorabbin Airport
to be converted to non-aviation land, however these concerns
generally did not relate to competition issues arising from the
Acquisition.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings:
• there is limited horizontal overlap in relation to the interests of
the Investors in industrial land and the industrial land at the
Moorabbin Airport site. The Investors other than Goodman
(noting that Goodman’s interest in Moorabbin Airport
Corporation would reduce as a result of the Acquisition) have
limited investments in industrial land in Greater Melbourne, and
alternative industrial land would continue to be available
following the Acquisition; and
• there is limited horizontal overlap between the aviation services
supplied at Moorabbin Airport and the interests of the Investors
in other airports, as the other airports either focus on different
primary operations to Moorabbin Airport (that is, they are
focused on regular passenger transport rather than general
aviation services) or offer general aviation services outside of
Victoria.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Midea Imaging Technology (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. (the **Acquirer**) proposes to indirectly acquire 77.5% of the issued share capital of Esaote S.p.A. (**Esaote**) (the **Acquisition**). The Acquisition will result in Midea Group Co., Ltd. (**Midea**) owning 87.73% of the issued share capital of Esaote.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Midea Imaging Technology (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. (the Acquirer)
applied for a notification waiver in respect of its proposed indirect
acquisition of 77.5% of the share capital in Esaote S.p.A (Esaote), as
described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).
The Acquisition will result in Midea Group Co., Ltd. (Midea) owning
87.73% of the issued share capital of Esaote.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

Midea is a global technology group organised into several divisions,
including Smart Home, Industrial Technology, Intelligent Building
Technology, Robotics & Automation, Energy Solutions, Healthcare
and Smart Logistics, among others. The Acquisition concerns
Midea’s Healthcare division, which Midea operates mainly through
the Acquirer, and its listed affiliate Beijing Wandong Medical
Technology Co., Ltd. (Wandong).
Wandong supplies medical imaging products and services. It
supplies magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), computed tomography,
digital subtraction angiography, and digital radiography equipment,
primarily in mainland China.
The Acquirer also owns the business of Carestream Healthcare
International Company Limited (Carestream). Carestream is a
worldwide provider of medical imaging systems; X-ray imaging
systems for non-destructive testing; and printing and film products.
Esaote is an international medical technology group that operates in
the field of medical diagnostic equipment, primarily ultrasound
systems, open and dedicated MRI systems designed for
musculoskeletal and extremity imaging, and healthcare information
technology.
Midea and Esaote overlap in the supply of medical imaging systems
and equipment in Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers
that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening
of competition. In particular:
a. there is a small horizontal overlap in the supply of medical
imaging systems and equipment, and the increase in share
of supply arising from the Acquisition would be small
b. there is no horizontal overlap between the parties in the
various categories of medical imaging systems and
equipment products that each supply
c. there are several alternative suppliers of medical imaging
systems and equipment to customers in Australia
d. there is a low risk of foreclosure or other concerning vertical
or conglomerate effects resulting from exclusionary
conduct, bundling or tying of different medical imaging
systems and equipment products post-acquisition.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

determination_table_content.details:
MedHealth Pty Limited (MedHealth) applied for a notification waiver

compliance, record retrieval, document management and related

ExamWorks operates in Australia through MedHealth, which
provides a range of services to a variety of businesses and
consumers across the private and public sectors.
MedHealth’s Allied Health Services Division provides allied health
services to a range of patients including NDIS participants, private

In South Australia, MedHealth provides occupational therapy, speech
pathology, exercise physiology, and physiotherapy allied health
services.
The target, Connect, was founded in 2016 and is a multi-disciplinary
AHS provider operating in South Australia. Connect provides clinic
and community-based speech therapy, occupational therapy,
physiotherapy, positive behavioural support, psychology and podiatry
services across Adelaide and the Riverland.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. the parties’ combined share in the supply of allied health
services in South Australia is low and the increment arising
from the Acquisition would be small
b. there are numerous alternative suppliers of various allied
health services to customers in Adelaide and regions in
South Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.

controlled by Cognizant Technology Solutions Corporation
(Cognizant), applied for a notification waiver in respect of its
proposed acquisition of the share capital in Astreya Partners Inc.
(Astreya), as described in the transaction documents provided as
part of the application (the Acquisition).

Inc., an indirect wholly-owned subsidiary of Cognizant, will merge
with and into Astreya, with Astreya continuing as the surviving

including financial services and insurance, energy and resources,
and retail.
Astreya is a digital and IT infrastructure managed service provider
that is focused primarily on digital workplace services (though
audiovisual technology), as well as delivering cloud, AI and other
infrastructure managed services. Astreya is headquartered in
California in the United States and has a limited presence in
Australia.
Both Cognizant and Astreya supply IT infrastructure managed
services, such as cloud infrastructure, AI and workplace
collaboration services.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is a small horizontal overlap between Cognizant and
Astreya in the supply of IT infrastructure managed services
b. Astreya has a small presence in Australia
c. the presence of alternative suppliers of IT infrastructure
managed services in Australia.

Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.

competition.

effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Alcoa Gallium Holdings Pty Ltd (a wholly owned subsidiary of Alcoa
of Australia Limited (AoA)), the United States Department of War
(DOW), and the Australian Government (through Export Finance
Australia (EFA)) applied for a notification waiver in respect of their
proposed acquisition of:
• shares in a recently incorporated Australian special purpose
vehicle, United States-Australia Gallium Pty Ltd (United
States-Australia Gallium SPV), and
• an entitlement to a proportionate share of gallium produced
by a proposed gallium production plant next to AoA’s
Wagerup alumina refinery in Western Australia to be
developed, constructed and operated by an unincorporated
joint venture between United States-Australia Gallium SPV
and Japan Australia Gallium Associates Pty Ltd,
as described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

AoA is a major alumina and aluminium producer, and its main
operations are mining bauxite at two locations in the Darling Range,
Western Australia, refining bauxite into alumina at two refineries in
Western Australia, operating shipping terminals at Kwinana and
Bunbury in Western Australia and Portland in Victoria, and holding a
55% interest in the Portland aluminium smelter in western Victoria.
The DOW is a United States government agency.
EFA is the Australian Government’s export credit agency, whose
purpose is to support Australian export trade, overseas investment,
and infrastructure development that benefits Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application, and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular, the Acquisition seeks to introduce a new
producer and supplier of primary gallium in Australia and does not
involve the horizontal aggregation of any existing gallium production
capacity.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

events.title: AMG - Killara - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Philip Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Australian Meat Group Pty Ltd (AMG) proposes to acquire 100% of
the issued share capital, livestock inventory and general stock
inventory of Killara Feedlot Pty Ltd (Killara) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, AMG, is an independent Australian beef processor.
AMG operates two beef processing facilities, one in Dandenong,
Victoria and the other in Cootamundra, New South Wales. It supplies
beef to the wholesale domestic and export markets.
The target, Killara, is a wholly owned subsidiary of Elders Rural
Services Australia Limited, an Australian listed agricultural services
provider. Killara owns 1,185 ha of land on the edge of the Liverpool
Plains, approximately 70km south of Tamworth, New South Wales. It
operates a Tier 1 EPA accredited feedlot, grain processing facilities,
cropping operations and grass feed paddocks, and supplies fat
cattle from the feedlot to beef processors.

AMG and Killara both operate in the cattle and beef supply chain.
AMG sources some of its cattle from feedlots. AMG also puts young
feeder cattle on feed at feedlots under a fee for service
arrangement.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information submitted by third parties and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• AMG would not have the ability and incentive to foreclose rival
processors from accessing fat cattle as Killara represents a
small proportion of cattle slaughtered in Australia and there are
multiple alternative feedlots available for fat cattle procurement.
• AMG would not have the ability and incentive to foreclose rival
feedlots from supplying fat cattle because AMG represents a
small proportion of beef processing capacity in Australia and
there are multiple alternative buyers of fat cattle.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Certain affiliates of American Industrial Partners propose to acquire 100% of the outstanding shares of Avanos Medical, Inc. through a share sale.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
A-AV Holdco I, Inc, an affiliate of AIP, LLC (American Industrial
Partners), applied for a notification waiver in respect of its proposed
acquisition of 100% of the share capital in Avanos Medical, Inc.
(Avanos Medical), as described in the transaction documents
provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

American Industrial Partners is a middle-market private equity firm
based in New York, U.S, with a focus on acquisition and
improvement of industrial businesses. The ADDMAN group
(ADDMAN) is the only American Industrial Partners portfolio
company which supplies products with applications in the medical
technology sector in Australia.
Avanos Medical is a US-headquartered medical device business
focused on clinically differentiated solutions in two segments:
Specialty Nutrition Systems and Pain Management & Recovery.
Speciality nutrition systems includes a range of enteral feeding
products and services designed to deliver nutrition into the patient’s
gastrointestinal tract and support care throughout the entire patient
journey.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is no horizontal overlap between American Industrial
Partners (including its connected entities) and Avanos
Medical in the supply of specialty nutrition systems or pain
management and recovery products
b. American Industrial Partners’ portfolio company ADDMAN, a
provider of medical manufacturing solutions, has a small
presence in Australia
c. there are alternative suppliers of speciality nutrition systems
and pain management and recovery products in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.
